mnemonics: Brute Force = Try Every Door. Imagine a security guard trying every single key on a huge keyring to find the one that unlocks a door.
keyPoints: It is a straightforward approach that solves a problem based directly on the problem statement and definitions of the concepts involved.
It is often the first algorithm that comes to mind due to its simplicity and lack of requirement for complex design.
Its main drawback is inefficiency, as it often has extremely poor time complexity (e.g., O(n!) for the brute-force Traveling Salesman Problem).
commonMistakes: Assuming brute force is never acceptable; it can be practical for very small input sizes or when it is the only known solution.
Confusing brute force with backtracking; backtracking is a more refined technique that prunes the search space, while pure brute force does not.
Overlooking the simplicity and clarity of a brute force solution when prototyping or proving that a solution exists.
